macro_segments.title: Rav Chisda's Ruling on Damages to Priestly Gifts and the Principle of Mamon She'ein Lo Tov'in

The Concept of Mamon She'ein Lo Tov'in: Property Without Individual Claimants
Ownership vs. Obligation: The Dispute Between Ownership and Mitzvah Alone

macro_segments.title: The Case of Eating Tevel Without Separating Terumot U'Ma'asrot

The Gemara's Narrow Ukimta: Tevel in Hands of Owner vs. Tevel in Hands of Kohen
Tosafot's Challenge: Why Does Separation Matter More Than Possession?

macro_segments.title: The Case of Royal Seizure of Grain and the Principle of Mishtarshe Leh

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: King Seizes Grain, Depriving the Levi of Ma'aser
Real Debt vs. Unjust Seizure: Obligation to Compensate Only When Debt Is Legitimate
The Concept of Mishtarshe Leh: Benefit That Takes Root and Requires Return
Tosafot's Distinction: Why Eating Differs From Benefit-Taking in Debt Situations

macro_segments.title: The Case of Purchasing Animal Organs from a Butcher and Priestly Gifts in Purchased Items

micro_segments.title: The Braitah: Buyer of Organs Must Give Stomach (Keiva) to Kohen
Flat Price vs. Purchase by Weight: Different Rules for Refund Rights
The Gemara's Question: Why Must Gifts Be Given If Ownership Has Transferred?
The Answer: Gifts Must Be Given While They Still Exist, Regardless of Possession

macro_segments.title: The Nine Gifts of the Kohen and the Meaning of Nichsei Kohen

micro_segments.title: The Nine Gifts: Terumah, Terumas Ma'aser, Challah, Reishis HaGez, and Others
The Question: Does Nichsei Kohen Imply Full Ownership Even Before Transfer?
The Answer: Nichsei Kohen Indicates Freedom of Use, Not Pre-Transfer Liability

macro_segments.title: The Levi Who Grabbed Priestly Gifts and the Debate Over Leviyim in the Term Am

micro_segments.title: The Incident: A Levi Forcibly Takes Priestly Gifts from Animals He Slaughtered
Rav's Complaint: Not Enough That He Keeps His Own; He Grabs Others' Gifts
The Question: Are Leviyim Called Am and Thus Obligated to Give Priestly Gifts?
Rav's Doubt: Whether Leviyim Are Obligated or Exempt From Giving Gifts

macro_segments.title: Priestly Gifts (Kohen Gifts) vs. Gifts to the Poor: Different Enforcement Rules

micro_segments.title: The Rav Idi Bar Abin Question: Using Precedent From Poor's Gifts to Determine Levi Status

macro_segments.title: Gifts to the Poor: Gifts of the Field and Maaser Ani With Their Different Rules

micro_segments.title: Four Vineyard Gifts: Peret, Olelot, Shichcha, and Pe'ah—No Owner's Right
The Principle: All Field Gifts Have No Tovat Hana'ah—Owner Cannot Choose Recipient
Poor Person's Obligation: Even the Poor Must Give These Gifts to Other Poor
Maaser Ani in the House: Owner Has Tovat Hana'ah and Chooses Recipient

macro_segments.title: Priestly Gifts (Kohen Gifts) vs. Gifts to the Poor: Different Enforcement Rules (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Contrast: Kohen Gifts Have No Tovat Hana'ah—Cannot Force One Kohen to Give Another
The Implication: Does This Mean Leviyim Must Give to Kohanim?

macro_segments.title: Gifts to the Poor: Gifts of the Field and Maaser Ani With Their Different Rules (continued)

micro_segments.title: Three Field Gifts: Leket, Shichcha, and Pe'ah—Scriptural Sources
Two Tree Gifts: Shichcha and Pe'ah Only—No Droppings
The Lesson of Obligation: Why Even the Poorest Must Participate in Giving

macro_segments.title: Reading the Braitah on Kohen vs. Levi Obligations: Attempts to Narrow the Case

micro_segments.title: Second Interpretation: The Case Is Ma'aser Rishon Post-Ezra's Fine of Leviyim
First Interpretation: The Case Is Not Zeroa Lechayayim Vekeiva but Reishis HaGez
Final Interpretation: The Case Concerns Things Without Kedusha After Ezra

macro_segments.title: Direct Proof That Leviyim Are Obligated: The Case of Shechting for a Levi vs. Shechting for a Non-Jew

micro_segments.title: The Braitah: Shechting for a Kohen or Non-Jew Exempts You; Shechting for Levi or Yisrael Obligates You
The Gemara's Corrective Reading: The Obligation Is Only for Yisrael, Not Necessarily Levi
The Counter: If Levi Were Exempt, the Braitah Would Have Stated It Explicitly

macro_segments.title: Tannaitic Dispute Over Whether Leviyim Are Included in the Term Am

micro_segments.title: The Scriptural Basis: Yom Kippur Atonement Verse Distinguishes Kohanim, Am, and Leviyim
The Tanaitic Debate: One Tana Says Leviyim Are in Am; Another Says They Need a Separate Word
The Gemara's Conclusion: Rav Is Uncertain Whether Leviyim Are Obligated, So We Don't Force Them
